design_philosophy.core_loop: Harvest spice, build base infrastructure, train faction-specific units, destroy enemy forces to progress campaign and attract sub-faction allies
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Multi-front RTS resource management with dynamic faction diplomacy system that rewards or penalizes player choices, affecting unit availability and story progression
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command one of three rival Great Houses competing for imperial supremacy on Arrakis, with mechanically distinct playstyles and branching narrative consequences
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Each of three playable Houses has entirely unique unit rosters and abilities (Atreides: sonic weapons/mechs, Harkonnen: tanks/flame, Ordos: energy shields/self-healing), forcing fundamentally different strategic approaches. Story branches based on tactical choices that impact sub-faction allegiances, making narrative emerge from gameplay decisions rather than linear exposition.

difficulty.continues_system: Campaign persistence across missions with dynamic map impact; player choices in earlier missions affect subsequent mission design and available allies
difficulty.checkpointing: Mission-based structure with persistent campaign state across chapters
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High strategic agency through unit composition choices, building placement, and diplomatic decisions with sub-factions that unlock or restrict unit access. Players can capture enemy buildings to acquire opposing faction units, adding dynamic counter-play options.

progression.upgrade_system: Sub-faction alliance system grants access to special units (Fremen, Ix, Tleilaxu, Guild, Imperial Sardaukar) based on campaign choices and diplomatic success. Enemy building capture unlocks alternative unit types mid-mission.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story-driven sub-faction recruitment through mission objectives and dialogue choices; mechanical unlocks tied to base capture and faction reputation
replayability_hooks: Three distinct single-player campaigns with unique unit rosters and story arcs
House Harkonnen campaign features binary choice outcome (Gunseng vs Copec succession) affecting late-game narrative and available units
Multiplayer skirmish and online modes with computer opponents
Dynamic map progression where earlier tactical decisions reshape subsequent mission environments and available alliances

world_building_techniques: Full Motion Video cutscenes with returning actors from Dune 2000, establishing character continuity
Campaign-specific subplot arcs (Atreides: Fremen reconciliation; Harkonnen: dynastic succession conflict; Ordos: ghola emperor manipulation)
Environmental storytelling through mission briefings that establish Arrakis political landscape post-Emperor Corrino assassination
Emergent narrative through faction choice mechanics that gate story branches and unit availability
Overarching meta-plot: Spacing Guild orchestrates 'war of assassins' to select new Padishah Emperor; Tleilaxu conspiracy to create psychic Emperor Worm revealed in endgame

boss_design.philosophy: Campaign-scale objective design rather than traditional boss encounters. Final confrontation involves destroying the Emperor Worm, a genetically-engineered strategic threat empowered by Lady Elara and controlled by Spacing Guild. Endgame requires resource mobilization and infiltration via Smugglers Guild to breach Guild-controlled territory.
notable_bosses: Emperor Worm (final boss): Psychically-empowered genetic creation requiring coordinated assault after guild-imposed stranding on conquered enemy homeworld
Opposing House final battles: Conclude with confrontation against player-selected rival House on their homeworld before Spacing Guild intervention
Harkonnen succession conflict: Baron Rakan's death triggers faction split between Gunseng and Copec with player-determined outcome affecting House command structure

faction_indicators: Three Great Houses with distinct ideological profiles: Atreides (benevolent, honor-bound), Harkonnen (ruthless, power-hungry), Ordos (manipulative, technologically amoral)
Sub-faction allegiance system (Fremen, Ix, Tleilaxu, Guild, Sardaukar) tracks player diplomatic standing and rewards/penalizes through unit access
Campaign choice system where player decisions to aid or betray sub-factions create branching relationship trees
Harkonnen internal schism between brothers reflects themes of succession and kinetic betrayal
danger_signals: Tleilaxu presence as hidden antagonist: genetic manipulation, flesh harvesting from sandworms, conspiracy to engineer psychic mind-control weapon
Spacing Guild revealed as orchestrator of imperial succession conflict; uses chemical mind-influencing drugs to enslave defeated forces
Lady Elara (Emperor's concubine) positioned as power-broker and co-conspirator in Emperor Worm plot
Ordos campaign explicitly frames House as creation of puppet emperor through genetic cloning, depicting technological overreach and political puppetry
environmental_storytelling: Arrakis as contested resource-extraction site: spice mélange economy drives all faction conflict and imperial succession
Mission-specific environmental changes reflect campaign consequences: conquered territories reshape subsequent mission battlefields
Base building and infrastructure placement as implicit faction characterization through unit types and defensive structures
Fog of war and black shroud map mechanics reinforce intelligence/reconnaissance theme central to 'war of assassins' framing
